title: The Perceptron: Modeling a Neuron with Math
concept: In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts built the first math model of a brain cell, and in 1958 Frank Rosenblatt turned that idea into the Perceptron — the first artificial neuron that could learn its own rules from examples instead of being hard-wired by a human.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When you built logic gates like AND and OR by hand, who decided the exact rule the gate followed?

options.text: A human programmer, who hard-wired the rule in advance
The gate learned the rule itself from examples
The rule was chosen randomly each time the gate ran

exercises.explain: Right! Every gate you built was hard-wired — a person set the rule ahead of time. Today, that changes.

cards.title: Can a machine write its own rules?
cards.body: So far, every logic gate you've built has had its rules hard-wired by a human — you decided exactly when AND or OR should fire. But what if a machine could look at examples and figure out the rule itself? In 1943, that exact question led two scientists to build the first math model of a brain cell.

cards.title: What is a perceptron?
cards.body: A perceptron is the simplest kind of artificial neuron: a small math unit that takes several inputs, weighs how important each one is, adds them up, and decides whether to "fire" (output 1) or stay quiet (output 0) — a rough math sketch of how one brain cell behaves.

cards.title: A neuron is like a tiny voting booth
cards.body: Picture a voting booth where several signals cast a vote, but not every vote counts the same — some voters (inputs) have more influence (weight) than others. If the weighted votes add up to enough support, the booth "fires" a yes decision. That's exactly what a perceptron does with numbers instead of people.

cards.title: Two milestones, fifteen years apart
cards.body: In 1943, neuroscientist Warren McCulloch and logician Walter Pitts published a paper describing a brain cell in pure math — a unit that fires only when enough signals arrive together. Fifteen years later, in 1958, Frank Rosenblatt built on that idea to create the Perceptron — the first artificial neuron that could automatically learn its own weights from examples, instead of a human setting them by hand.

cards.title: Not a full brain, and not brand new
cards.body: It's tempting to picture a perceptron as a mini brain, or assume it's some recent invention. Neither is true: it's a single, simple decision-making unit, not a whole thinking machine — and it's nearly 70 years old. Rosenblatt built the first one back in 1958, long before modern computers existed.

left.text:
A human sets the rule
Fixed forever
Never adapts

right.text:
Learns weights from examples
Can be retrained
Adapts to new data

exercises.prompt: Who published the 1943 paper describing the first math model of a brain cell?
exercises.explain: Correct — McCulloch (a neuroscientist) and Pitts (a logician) teamed up in 1943 to describe a simplified brain cell using pure math.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In 1958, Frank Rosenblatt built the Perceptron, the first artificial neuron that could automatically learn its own weights from examples.
exercises.explain: True! That's exactly what made the Perceptron a breakthrough — it wasn't just modeled in math, it was actually built, and it could learn.

exercises.sentence: The main difference between a hard-wired gate and a perceptron is that a perceptron can ___ its rule from examples instead of a human setting it.
exercises.explain: Exactly — 'learn' is the key word. A perceptron adjusts its own weights based on examples instead of a human hard-wiring the rule.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes what a perceptron does?

options.text: It takes weighted inputs, adds them up, and fires if the sum crosses a threshold
It stores every example it has ever seen and looks them up like a dictionary
It randomly guesses an output with no relationship to its inputs

exercises.explain: Right — a perceptron's whole job is to weigh, sum, and compare against a threshold. We'll break that process down in the next lesson.

exercises.prompt: You just learned that in 1958, Rosenblatt's Perceptron could learn its own weights, instead of a human hard-wiring a gate's rule by hand like in the previous unit. What's the key idea that made this such a big leap forward?

options.text: A perceptron could adjust its own weights from examples, so a human no longer had to hand-set every rule
A perceptron could think exactly like a human brain, with full understanding
A perceptron replaced the need for any inputs at all
A perceptron only worked if a human typed in the final answer directly

exercises.explain: Exactly! The leap wasn't that Rosenblatt built something conscious — it's that the Perceptron could learn from examples, adjusting its own weights instead of a human hard-wiring them. Next: how a perceptron actually decides when to fire, using weighted sums and a threshold.
